message.content:
## Source Extractor

Research question: "DESIGNING AND TRAINING A VOICE / TTS MODEL TO BE MORE COMPREHENSIBLE AT HIGH-SPEED LISTENING. Deliver BOTH the underlying science AND concrete recommendations for a local open-source TTS podcast pipeline (currently uses Kokoro TTS). Cover these 6 angles: (1) ACOUSTIC/PROSODIC PROPERTIES that survive time-compression / make a voice intelligible when sped up — "clear speech"/hyperarticulation, vowel-space expansion, consonant emphasis/sharper consonant-vowel transitions, modulation-spectrum shape, F0/pitch characteristics, and pause/rhythm structure. (2) FORMANT SYNTHESIS vs NEURAL TTS at extreme speed — why blind screen-reader power users overwhelmingly prefer old formant synthesizers (Eloquence, DECtalk, eSpeak-NG) over natural neural voices at 18-22 syllables/second; consistency, crispness, latency, predictability; quantify the naturalness-vs-intelligibility trade-off. (3) RATE-AWARE / CONTROLLABLE TTS GENERATION — conditioning or fine-tuning a neural TTS to NATIVELY generate fast-but-clear speech (speaking-rate control, prosody control, training on clear-speech/Lombard-speech corpora) vs. post-hoc time-scale modification; CRITICAL: how much MEASURABLE comprehensibility gain does native fast generation or fine-tuning actually buy over simply time-compressing a normal voice? (the user will only invest in fine-tuning/training if the gain is significant). (4) DSP ENHANCEMENT for intelligibility at speed — formant/clarity enhancement, spectral shaping + dynamic-range compression, consonant boosting, content-aware PER-PHONEME variable-rate compression (compress steady-state vowels more, preserve consonant transitions), and modulation-envelope enhancement; these apply to ANY engine's output. (5) CONCRETE ENGINES/VOICES & CONFIG KNOBS — head-to-head comparison of which TTS engines/voices are most intelligible at high speed (Kokoro, Piper, eSpeak-NG/Eloquence, RHVoice, Azure/Google neural, ElevenLabs) and the tunable settings (speaking rate, pitch, pause length) that improve speed-comprehensibility. (6) HOW TO MEASURE "comprehensibility at speed" — objective intelligibility metrics (STOI, STMI/spectro-temporal modulation index, modulation-spectrum analysis) and practical subjective A/B / MUSHRA listening-test protocols, so one can decide whether a fine-tune cleared a "significant gain" bar before committing. CONSTRAINTS/CONTEXT: pipeline runs LOCALLY/on-device (open-source Kokoro), so favor approaches compatible with local inference. Output should serve TWO use cases: a normal natural-sounding default voice for general students, AND an optional aggressively speed-optimized track for trained power listeners (robotic is acceptable for that track if measurably clearer). For EACH effort tier (pick/configure a voice < DSP post-processing < fine-tune an open model < train from scratch), state the expected comprehensibility gain, the cost/effort, and whether it is worth it. Prefer peer-reviewed and authoritative sources; include practitioner/community knowledge from the blind/screen-reader speed-listening community where relevant."

Fetch and extract key claims from this source:
**URL:** https://rdouglasfields.com/2010/12/14/extraordinary-ability-of-blind-people-to-hear-ultrafast-speech/
**Title:** Extraordinary Ability of Blind People to Hear Ultrafast Speech (R. Douglas Fields, Scientific American contributor)
**Found via:** formant-synth-vs-neural/blind-power-users search

## Task
1. Use WebFetch to retrieve the page content.
2. Assess source quality: primary research/institution? secondary reporting? blog/opinion? forum? unreliable?
3. Extract 2-5 FALSIFIABLE claims that bear on the research question. Each claim must:
   - be a concrete, checkable statement (not vague generalities)
   - include a direct quote from the source as support
   - be rated central/supporting/tangential to the research question
4. Note publish date if available.

If the fetch fails or the page is irrelevant/paywalled, return claims: [] and sourceQuality: "unreliable".

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll fetch the page content and extract relevant claims.

input.prompt: Extract all concrete, falsifiable claims about: (1) the speed at which blind people can comprehend speech (syllables per second, words per minute, or compression factor vs normal); (2) how this compares to sighted listeners' limits; (3) any neuroscience findings about brain regions (e.g., visual cortex) recruited for ultrafast speech processing; (4) any details about the type of speech synthesis used (formant synthesis, screen readers, text-to-speech engines) and acoustic properties that make ultrafast speech intelligible. For each claim, give the exact quote from the article. Also report the publish date and author.

content.content:
# Concrete Claims Extract: Ultrafast Speech Comprehension in Blind Individuals

**Publication Date:** December 14, 2010  
**Author:** R. Douglas Fields, Ph.D.

---

## (1) Speed of Blind Comprehension

**Claim:** Maximum processing rate  
> "Blind people in the study could hear speech as fast as 25 syllables a second!"

**Claim:** Individual variation  
> "some blind people I know confirmed that the 24 syllables per second sample is at their upper limit"

**Claim:** Routine material at higher speeds  
> "I can recognize phrases I hear from my screen reader on a regular basis at those speeds...I can comprehend that stuff at much higher speeds"

---

## (2) Comparison to Sighted Listeners

**Claim:** Sighted baseline  
> "I am sighted and max out already at the 14 sps sample"

**Claim:** General superiority  
> "blind people can understand speech at ultrafast rates, well beyond what a sighted person can comprehend"

---

## (3) Neuroscience Findings

**Claim:** Cortical rewiring mechanism  
> "The parts of the brain that process hearing get re-wired to the part of the cerebral cortex that normally handles vision"

---

## (4) Speech Synthesis & Acoustic Properties

**Claim:** Formant synthesis advantage  
> "I, and I know I'm not the only one, find formant synths easier to comprehend when speeded up than concatenated ones"

**Claim:** Pitch preservation effects  
> "My digital talking book player allows me to speed up the voices of the human narrators without changing the pitch"

content.text:
The fetch succeeded and returned substantive content directly relevant to the research question, particularly angles (1), (2), and the formant-synth-vs-neural preference.

This is a science blog post by R. Douglas Fields (a credentialed neuroscientist and Scientific American contributor), but the most useful technical claims come from reader comments by blind screen-reader users rather than from the author's own peer-reviewed work — so this is best classified as a blog with practitioner/community knowledge embedded.

claims.claim: Blind listeners in a referenced study could comprehend speech at rates up to ~25 syllables per second, far beyond the sighted limit.
claims.quote: Blind people in the study could hear speech as fast as 25 syllables a second!

claims.claim: Sighted listeners max out at much lower speech rates than blind power-listeners, with a sighted baseline around 14 syllables/second versus 24+ for blind users.
claims.quote: I am sighted and max out already at the 14 sps sample

claims.claim: Blind screen-reader users find formant synthesizers easier to comprehend when sped up than concatenative (neural/natural) synthesizers.
claims.quote: I, and I know I'm not the only one, find formant synths easier to comprehend when speeded up than concatenated ones

claims.claim: In blindness, auditory processing regions of the brain get re-wired to recruit the cerebral cortex that normally handles vision, supporting ultrafast speech comprehension.
claims.quote: The parts of the brain that process hearing get re-wired to the part of the cerebral cortex that normally handles vision

claims.claim: Time-scale modification that speeds up speech without changing pitch is used by talking-book players to make ultrafast narration intelligible.
claims.quote: My digital talking book player allows me to speed up the voices of the human narrators without changing the pitch

content.text: Source extracted. The page is a 2010 science blog post by neuroscientist R. Douglas Fields, with the most load-bearing technical claims (the 25 sps figure, sighted 14 sps baseline, and formant-vs-concatenative preference) coming from blind screen-reader users in the discussion — classified as `blog` with embedded practitioner/community knowledge. Key central claims support research angles (1) acoustic/intelligibility limits and (2) formant-synth-vs-neural preference among blind power listeners. Findings returned via StructuredOutput.
